title: SEGMENT 18 — Single-cell, spatial, and in vivo methylation — the frontier

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Single-cell, spatial, and in vivo methylation — the frontier

We've spent most of this lecture on bulk methylation analysis — methods that average methylation across the millions of cells in a tissue sample or the billions of cell-free DNA molecules in a plasma draw.
The frontier of the methylation field is moving beyond bulk, into spatially resolved, single-cell, and in vivo dynamic methylation measurement.
Let me sketch the three main frontier directions before we close.
Frontier one: single-cell methylation.
The first single-cell bisulfite sequencing was published in 2014 by the Reik lab at the Babraham Institute, demonstrating that you could in principle get a methylation profile from a single cell.
The technique was lossy and noisy — bisulfite damage at single-cell scale is brutal, and each single cell only contributes coverage at a small fraction of the genome's CpGs — but it worked.
Subsequent improvements (snmC-seq, snmC-seq2, scNMT-seq, scBS-seq) have refined the chemistry, and the introduction of EM-seq-based single-cell protocols has dramatically improved data quality.

By 2026, single-cell methylation profiling is a real, if expensive, technique, with datasets of tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands of cells being generated for tissue methylation atlas projects.
The biological yield is substantial.

Single-cell methylation has revealed methylation heterogeneity within tissues that bulk methods can't see — different cell types within a tumor, different cells in different states within a developmental compartment, rare cell populations with distinctive methylation programs.

The brain methylation atlases produced by Joe Ecker's lab and others using single-nucleus methylation sequencing have revealed the methylation diversity of neuronal subtypes at unprecedented resolution.
Combined with single-cell ATAC-seq and single-cell RNA-seq, single-cell methylation gives a multi-modal view of cell state that the bulk era couldn't approach.
Frontier two: spatial methylation.

Just as spatial transcriptomics has revolutionized how we measure gene expression in tissue context, spatial methylation aims to map methylation patterns across the physical landscape of a tissue section.

The early techniques here are still relatively primitive — most rely on capturing DNA from spatial barcoded slides similar to the Visium platform, with all the resolution and sensitivity limitations that implies — but progress is rapid.

Spatial methylation methods like spatial-CUT&Tag for histone modifications, and the methylation analogs being developed by Rong Fan's lab at Yale and others, are pushing toward a future where you can see methylation patterns across a tissue section at near-cellular resolution.

This will matter enormously for tumor biology, where methylation heterogeneity within a tumor maps onto spatial structure, and for development, where methylation gradients across embryonic tissues encode positional information.
Frontier three: in vivo dynamic methylation.
All the methods we've discussed measure methylation at a single time point — you take a sample, you process it, you read out a snapshot.
But methylation is dynamic.
It changes during development, during cell-state transitions, in response to environmental signals.
Measuring those dynamics in living cells, without disturbing them, is technically very hard, but it's the frontier.
Methods like CRISPR-based methylation imaging — fluorescent tagging of methylation readers to localize methylated regions in live cells — are early-stage but exciting.

Methods that use metabolic labeling of newly synthesized DNA, combined with methylation profiling, can distinguish "old" methylation patterns from "new" ones and reveal methylation dynamics during cell proliferation.
The full vision — watching methylation change in real time at single-locus resolution in living cells — is not yet realized, but the pieces are starting to come together.
There's also a fourth direction worth flagging — direct in vivo writing of methylation.
Engineered DNA methyltransferases fused to CRISPR Cas9 nickase or dead Cas9 can be targeted to specific genomic sites, where they deposit methylation marks.
The same can be done with TET enzymes to deposit demethylation.
This is the methylation-editing toolkit, and it's being used both as a research tool to test causal hypotheses about methylation function and as a potential therapeutic modality.
If aberrant methylation drives a disease — silencing of a tumor suppressor, for example — targeted demethylation might rescue it.
Clinical applications are very early, but the technology is real and improving.
Methylation, then, is no longer a static, hard-to-measure mark.

It's a dynamic, increasingly measurable, increasingly manipulable layer of biological information that overlaps with development, with disease, with aging, with environmental response, with cellular identity.

The methods we've been building up through this lecture — from Frommer's 1992 bisulfite conversion to nanopore current-based calling and CRISPR methylation editing — are the toolkit that has made all of this possible.

If you walk out of here with one thing, let it be this: methylation is the most measurable, most studied, most clinically actionable layer of the epigenome, and the measurement methods are still evolving rapidly.
The bisulfite era is winding down.
The enzymatic and nanopore era is just beginning.
The single-cell, spatial, and in vivo era is the frontier.
And whatever comes next will continue to enrich how we read this small chemical tag that does so much biological work.
Thank you.
Take a few minutes to stretch, and then we can take questions.
